content: Every compound German number is built from 20 base numbers. These must be automatic before anything else.
Numbers 1-12 are irregular and must be memorised. From 13 onwards a pattern begins: dreizehn, vierzehn, fünfzehn — the units come first, then -zehn (teen).

german: eins, zwei, drei, vier, fünf, sechs, sieben, acht, neun, zehn, elf, zwölf
english: one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, nine, ten, eleven, twelve

text: eins, zwei, drei, vier, fünf, sechs, sieben, acht, neun, zehn, elf, zwölf

german: dreizehn, vierzehn, fünfzehn, sechzehn, siebzehn, achtzehn, neunzehn, zwanzig
english: thirteen, fourteen, fifteen, sixteen, seventeen, eighteen, nineteen, twenty

text: dreizehn, vierzehn, fünfzehn, sechzehn, siebzehn, achtzehn, neunzehn, zwanzig

content: The Goethe examiner will use numbers in the Hören section. You must recognise them instantly — not translate them.
Common mistake: sechzehn (not sechszehn) and siebzehn (not siebenzehn). The s and en are dropped.
